title: Chicken Biryani
tagline: Diet-friendly, chicken-heavy dum biryani scaled up from a packet mix for 1 kg chicken.
servingsNote: Chicken-heavy — lighter on rice per portion.
macroNotes: Estimated for 1 kg curry-cut bone-in chicken + 300 g raw rice + 200 g curd + 1 onion + 1 tbsp oil + 1 tbsp ghee + packet mix. Bone-in inflates the raw weight — actual edible meat is ~650-700 g, so per-plate protein is lower than the 1 kg suggests.
Swap to 1 kg chicken BREAST and the whole pot becomes roughly ~1900-2100 kcal / ~230-250 g protein with only ~35-45 g fat — bodybuilder macros while still eating real biryani.
Air-fry the onions instead of deep-frying (160-170C, 12-18 min, light oil spray) to save ~80-120 kcal with almost all the flavour.

Keeping rice low (300 g for 1 kg chicken vs the packet's 200 g per 500 g) is the main lever that makes this diet-friendly — go down to 250 g if you want it even more chicken-forward, though 300 g stops it turning wet and pulao-like.

items: 1 kg curry-cut bone-in chicken (skin removed), or breast for a leaner version
1 tsp salt (packet is already ~33% salt — go easy)
300 g basmati rice (~1.5 cups), washed and soaked 20-30 min

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.body: Mix 1 kg chicken with 200 g curd, the full 50 g biryani packet, 1 tbsp MDH Chicken Masala, 2 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, 1 tbsp lemon juice, 1 tsp Kashmiri chilli, 1/2 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp cumin powder, 1/2 tsp garam masala and 1 tsp salt. Rest at least 30 min, ideally 2-4 hours. Taste the marinade for salt before cooking — the packet already carries a lot.

steps.title: Parboil the rice to 80%
steps.body: Boil the soaked 300 g rice in plenty of salted water until 80% cooked — the grain should still have a firm core. Drain. Cooking it to 80% now means the dum finishes it perfectly without going mushy.

steps.title: Fry the onions
steps.body: Air-fry 1 sliced onion at 170C for 12-15 min (shake every 4-5 min) or fry in 1 tbsp oil until deep golden. Reserve half for garnish. Air-frying saves ~80-120 kcal.

steps.title: Steep the saffron milk
steps.body: Warm 75 ml milk, crush 12-15 saffron strands into it with an optional pinch of sugar, and let it sit 15-20 min while you cook.

steps.title: Cook the chicken masala
steps.body: Heat the pot with 1 tbsp ghee (plus 1 tbsp oil if the onions were air-fried). Add all the marinated chicken and cook on medium-high 12-15 minutes until it's ~70-80% cooked, the gravy has thickened and oil just starts to separate. If it's watery, cook 5 more minutes uncovered — you want a thick masala layer, not a curry. Stir in half the fried onions and 2/3 of the mint and coriander.

steps.title: Layer
steps.body: Spread the chicken evenly across the bottom. Layer all the 80% cooked rice on top — do NOT mix. Scatter the remaining fried onions, remaining 1/3 mint and coriander, then drizzle all the saffron milk evenly over the surface and dot with 1 tsp ghee.

steps.title: Dum
steps.body: Cover tightly (wrap foil around the rim or put a damp cloth under the lid if it's loose). Cook 5 minutes on medium, then 15 minutes on low, then turn the heat OFF and rest 10 minutes undisturbed — don't open it. If the chicken gravy looked wet before layering, do 5 min medium + 20 min low instead.

steps.title: Serve
steps.body: Open the lid, and with a spoon gently lift portions from the bottom up rather than stirring — you want bites with white rice, saffron rice and chicken masala mixed naturally.

notes: The packet is built for 500 g chicken + 200 g rice. Scaled over 1 kg chicken + 300 g rice, the extra 1 tbsp MDH Chicken Masala + 1/2 tsp garam masala + 1/2 tsp chilli powder are what stop the chicken tasting under-seasoned.
1 kg 'curry cut' in India usually means bone-in, skin off — only ~650-700 g is actual edible meat, so don't be fooled by the raw weight when counting protein.
Onions add sweetness, body and aroma. If you cut them entirely, compensate with extra mint, extra coriander and 1 tbsp lemon juice.
300 g rice is the sweet spot for 1 kg chicken — 250 g goes too wet and meaty, more than 300 g dilutes the diet benefit.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
